results.possibleIdentity: Martha Turi (1918–2015), also known as Martha Turi Tauss, was a Hungarian-born artist, designer, and plastic consultant active in New York City during the 1960s and 1970s. She served as the Director of Designs & Sales at Just Plastics Incorporated, a New York-based firm that specialized in acrylic and plastic fabrication. Turi was a notable participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, where she was involved in the Technical Services Program, which matched artists with technical experts. Her professional and creative work centered on the innovative use of plastics; she designed furniture, such as coffee tables and see-through pieces, as well as acrylic sculptures and wall hangings. Her designs were featured in major New York retailers like Macy's and Gimbels in the late 1960s. Additionally, she collaborated on industrial and fashion projects, including a transparent plastic handbag with Lilly Rotkel of Delill Handbags. Her contributions to the field are documented in the E.A.T. records at the Getty Research Institute, which include recordings of her technical consultations.
